title: Will CPI rise more than 0.1% in June 2026?

current.lean_note: no +EV value bet at the ask (my prob 0%, modal NO) — staying out.
current.rationale_summary: The evidence suggests that inflation has already peaked in May 2026, with energy prices falling in June, which historically has a significant impact on CPI. Additionally, the Netherlands and Italy have reported slowing inflation rates in June 2026, which supports the notion of a decline in overall inflationary pressure. However, the question is about whether CPI rises more than 0.1% in June 2026,
reference_classes: historical energy price impact on CPI

history.lean_note: ADVERSARIAL VETO: Forecaster's edge depends on unproven assumptions about geopolitical events and energy markets, diverging from liquid market pricing. Confidence is inflated relative to market odds, and reasoning neglects base rates and alternative inflatio
adversarial_concerns: Edge reality questionable: reliance on speculative 'post-ceasefire energy reversal' without evidence of timing or likelihood; market implies higher probability of CPI rise despite forecaster's assumptions
Overconfidence in anchoring to 0.10 without robust justification for retail-gas 'downward stickiness' (no data cited)
Reasoning flaw: conjunction of two low-probability events (energy reversal + core firmness) without quantifying their joint likelihood
history.rationale_summary: >+0.1% needs energy NOT to reverse AND firm core — a conjunction against the central case (post-ceasefire energy reversal drags June headline toward zero/negative). Must be <= P(>0.0%)~0.125; anchor modestly below for retail-gas downward stickiness. ~0.10.

history.lean_note: no +EV value bet at the ask (my prob 12%, modal NO) — staying out.
history.rationale_summary: MoM >0.2% needs roughly flat-to-positive energy plus firm core; the documented ~13% gasoline drop makes that a clear tail.

history.lean_note: 77pt gap exceeds the hard ceiling (20pt) — beyond this divergence from a liquid market, even high confidence is treated as model error, not edge
history.rationale_summary: The base rate for monthly CPI increases is typically small, often in the range of 0.1% to 0.3% in normal economic conditions. However, given the current global economic environment, including potential inflationary pressures from supply chain disruptions, rising energy prices, and strong consumer demand, the likelihood of a CPI increase exceeding 0.1% in June 2026 is elevated. reference_classes: Inflation forecasts from major economic institutions
